title: Promoting well-doing: A Positive Psychology intervention for regulating performance momentum and thriving among high achievers
description: The present study examined a novel journalling intervention designed to support subjective wellbeing and perceived work performance among high achievers. Sixty-seven university students reporting a grade average of B+ or higher completed a one-week, within-subjects pre–post study involving a daily ‘well done’ journalling task focused on small actions done well. Subjective wellbeing and perceived work performance were measured pre- and post- intervention; with journalling frequency and qualitative feedback collected afterwards. Findings indicated no detectable change in wellbeing from an elevated baseline, however, a significant improvement in perceived work performance was observed. Post-hoc analyses revealed a dose–response pattern, with stronger performance improvements among participants with frequent task engagement. Qualitative feedback was generally favourable, suggesting perceived benefits in positive affect, self-esteem, and motivation. Overall, results show preliminary support for ‘well done’ journalling to enhance perceived performance in high-achieving students.

title: "It's a balancing act": Autistic fathers navigating parenting in everyday life
description:
Background
Autistic parents are increasingly recognized as an important source of insight into Autistic adulthood and family life, yet the perspectives of Autistic people who identify as fathers remain almost entirely absent from research. Fathers have distinct experiences, and the absence of their stories and perspectives limits understanding of Autistic parenting more broadly. This study addressed this gap by exploring how Autistic fathers understand and experience parenting across the lifespan.
Methods
We co-produced the study with four Autistic fathers who contributed to design, interview development, and analysis. Nineteen Autistic fathers took part in semi-structured interviews, either spoken or written, depending on their preference. We analyzed the data using reflexive thematic analysis informed by constructionist and participatory research principles.
Results
Our analysis generated five themes: (1) Finding joy and meaning in everyday moments, which captured the emotional rewards of fatherhood; (2) Becoming myself through fatherhood, describing how parenting strengthened self-understanding and Autistic identity; (3) Walking the tightrope of family life, reflecting the effort of balancing sensory, emotional, and practical demands; (4) Parenting as relational work, showing how fathers negotiated understanding and communication within family relationships and the tension that arose when they felt misunderstood; and (5) Parenting in systems stacked against us, highlighting the barriers encountered in services and wider society.
Conclusion
Autistic fathers’ accounts deepen understanding of Autistic adulthood and family life. Their experiences show that fatherhood can strengthen identity and connection while exposing the limits of systems that rarely recognize Autistic needs. These fathers’ insights call for change in how research, policy, and services engage with Autistic people and their families.

title: Metabolic connectivity
description: The human brain is a complex network, whose functional properties are constrained by its anatomy and energetic constraints. Metabolic connectivity refers to coherent metabolic interactions between spatially distinct regions, and is most commonly measured using [18F]-fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG) positron emission tomography (PET). While metabolic connectivity as a field predates fMRI-derived functional connectivity measures, it was not until recently that it was possible to estimate a metabolic network at the individual person level. Here, we review the theoretical and historical contexts of metabolic connectivity, its physiological bases, major methodological approaches, and key findings in health and disease. The review highlights that metabolic connectivity yields unique insight into the functional interactions of the brain relative to fMRI, is predictive of cognition and individual phenotype, and displays unique patterns of time-variant state switching and gradient architecture. We conclude the review with a forward-looking perspective of the future of metabolic connectivity, and recommendations for extending the conceptual framework of the measure.

title: The Impact of Race and Gender on Tenure and Promotion Decisions in STEM Careers
description: Gender and race disparities persist among tenure-track faculty in the United States, especially in STEM, where women hold 26% of academic positions and Black and Hispanic faculty together comprise only 7%. We conducted a randomized controlled experiment with 796 tenured biology and chemistry professors employed at 183 U.S. doctoral-granting institutions to investigate their evaluation of pre-tenure faculty's research readiness for tenure and promotion in research-intensive universities. Faculty received an identical CV with only the candidate’s name varying to signal their gender and race, using a 2 Race (Black vs. White) by 2 Gender (male, female) between-subjects factorial design. Results revealed that STEM faculty evaluated female and Black candidates' research record significantly more favorably and recommended them for tenure and promotion at higher rates, compared to male and White candidates with identical CVs. Findings remained largely consistent even after controlling for faculty participants’ own gender, race, and rank. We speculate that social norms about diversity, equity, and inclusion prominent in 2020-21 when these data were collected during the COVID-19 pandemic and shortly after George Floyd’s murder may have heightened faculty’s awareness of gender and race bias, increased motivation to be fair in decision-making, and led to compensatory judgments to increase diversity in STEM careers.

title: Time Regained: Decision Processes in Continuous-Outcome Retrieval from Visual Working Memory
description: Decision-making models of memory retrieval hold that across-trial variability in memory representations and within-trial variability in matching the contents of memory to the retrieval cue both contribute to variability in the speed and accuracy of retrieval decisions. In contrast, most recent studies of visual working memory (VWM) using continuous-outcome decision tasks, in which responses are made on continuous scales, do not distinguish between memory variability and decision-process variability in characterizing retrieval errors and are silent on decision times. We combined a successful model of continuous-outcome decisions, the circular diffusion model (CDM), with a peaked, heavy-tailed distribution of memory representations, the Jones-Pewsey distribution, to characterize the speed and accuracy of continuous-outcome decisions about the hues of saturated and desaturated color patches in four experiments that varied the difficulty of the decision task and the memory load of the VWM task. The CDM provided a good account of the marginal and joint distributions of response times and response errors and the anisotropies of stimuli in different parts of the color space across all experiments. VWM precision followed either an inverse square-root law or power law in set size, depending on saturation, as predicted by sample-size and attention-weighted sample-size models, respectively. The results support a model in which the capacity limits of VWM reflect the statistical properties of a population of noisy neurons that are divided among items in memory under attentional control and determine the precision of recalled items.

title: Do Emotional Faces Modulate Pupillary Pseudoneglect?
description: Pseudoneglect, a subtle attentional bias toward the left visual field in neurologically healthy individuals, can be measured with a split-screen method in which a bright stimulus in one hemifield elicits stronger pupil constriction when it appears on the left than on the right (Strauch et al., 2022). Replications of this method have reported mixed results, including a large Registered Report that replicated the effect with a small effect size (d =  0.31). Given strong left-field biases in the emotional chimeric face test, the present study (n = 53) combined this task with split-screen pupillometry to test whether emotional face processing amplifies pupillary pseudoneglect. Pupillary pseudoneglect was observed during control trials with non-faces (d =  0.36) and was stronger for emotional faces (d =  0.76), but this difference was not significant. A strong left-field bias was confirmed for emotional face judgements (d =  0.76), which did not correlate with pupillary bias (ρ =  0.26), and for a line bisection task (d =  0.47), which also did not correlate with pupillary pseudoneglect (ρ =  0.19). These data support the split-screen pupillometric method for measuring pseudoneglect and replicate a previously-noted pattern that leftward biases across tasks show little statistical relation to one another.

title: Population heterogeneity in economic preferences: Revisiting the Global Preference Survey
description: Falk et al. (2018a) documented substantial cross-country variation in economic preferences elicited in the Global Preference Survey (GPS), but it remains unclear how much of this variation reflects sampling noise rather than true differences across populations. We address this question by applying random-effects meta-analytic models to Falk et al.’s data from roughly 80,000 respondents in 76 countries, separately estimating the country-level heterogeneity in six preferences—patience, risk taking, positive and negative reciprocity, altruism, and trust—as well as in gender differences and in the association between subjective math skills and preferences. Between-country heterogeneity is about one-third of the individual-level standard deviation in economic preferences and increases to about one-half after adjusting for measurement error. Ten (bio-)geographical and cultural factors explain a nontrivial share of this heterogeneity only in patience, but not in the remaining preferences. Heterogeneity in gender differences and in the math-skills association is moderate to large, yet corresponds to only about a third of the heterogeneity in preference levels. To characterize what this heterogeneity implies for the generalizability of effects, we quantify the share of countries whose true effect is expected to lie in the same direction as the meta-analytic mean: this “sign generalizability” ranges from 67% (patience) to 97% (risk taking) for gender differences, and from 89% (negative reciprocity) to 100% (trust) for the math-skills–preferences association. Overall, our results confirm meaningful population-level variability in preferences while showing that the generalizability of gender and skill gradients across countries varies across the six references.

title: Quantum of Silence: The Burden of Imposed Secrecy
description: Secrecy is a pervasive feature of everyday life. While research has predominantly focused on secrets people choose to keep, the psychological mechanisms of imposed secrecy remain largely unexplored. The prototypical example of imposed secrecy is a professional secret—one that is organizationally mandated, with high stakes if leaked to the wrong ear, or at the wrong time. To better understand the burden of imposed secrecy, we propose a framework that integrates theoretical insights from multiple psychological subdisciplines and empirical evidence from applied professional settings. The framework foregrounds five key aspects of imposed secrecy: the degree of intrinsic and extrinsic motivation in keeping secrets (the why), secret content and appraisals (the what), secrecy processes (the how), individual differences (the who) and contextual factors (the where) that influence secrecy effectiveness and impact. Our framework allows for a nuanced examination of distinct and interrelated impacts on the well-being of personnel handling imposed secrets. This approach guides future research towards a comprehensive understanding of the costs and benefits of imposed secrecy, including professional secrets across diverse industries and disciplines. Ultimately, this work provides a foundation for the development of more effective ways to improve the well-being of people who are required to keep secrets.

title: Psychological features of dispute content and public acceptance of AI in legal adjudication: evidence for systematic variation beyond individual differences
description: Public acceptance of artificial intelligence (AI) in legal decision-making has been primarily explained through individual differences in personality traits and general attitudes toward technology. However, emerging evidence suggests that contextual features of legal disputes themselves may systematically influence preferences for AI versus human adjudicators. Across two studies with Japanese participants (N = 1,384 and N = 596), we examined whether psychological characteristics of dispute content—beyond demographics and individual traits—shape acceptability judgments for algorithmic adjudication. Study 1 employed exploratory factor analysis on acceptability ratings across 46 legal dispute vignettes, revealing a robust two-dimensional structure distinguishing interpersonal-relational disputes (where human adjudicators were strongly preferred) from institutional-procedural disputes (where AI acceptance was comparatively higher, though not surpassing human preference in most cases). Study 2 replicated this dimensional structure in an independent sample and demonstrated that experimentally manipulated contextual features—emotional involvement and prototypicality—systematically modulated acceptability judgments, with effects varying by dispositional trust, AI-specific attitudes, and gender. AI-specific expectations emerged as the strongest predictor of acceptance (η2 = 0.252), and a three-way interaction among emotional involvement, gender, and prototypicality indicated that contextual effects are moderated by individual characteristics. These findings suggest that the psychological features of dispute content constitute an overlooked dimension in AI acceptance research, extending beyond technology acceptance models to fundamental questions about how individuals construe social problems and allocate adjudicative authority. We discuss limitations related to measurement approaches, alternative psychological mechanisms, and directions for future research employing real-wor

title: Parent experiences of holistic early intervention for children with motor learning impairments: A reflexive thematic analysis of Dame Vera Lynn Children’s Charity case studies
description: Early intervention services are widely recommended to support children with motor learning impairments. These services are recommended to be family-centred, and support the parent alongside the child, rather than child at the expense of the parent. In absence of any qualitative reports of holistic early intervention programs within the UK healthcare system, we present our thematic analysis of case studies from Dame Vera Lynn Children’s Charity (DVLCC). We generated four themes of parent experiences: 1.) coming to the DVLCC in distress; 2.) holistic approach to support at DVLCC; 3.) improved child wellbeing; and 4.) improved parent wellbeing. Together, these themes present DVLCC support as a transformational experience for children and their families, supporting them with holistic, relational, family-orientated early intervention services where public health systems lack the resources. Future research is needed to qualitatively and quantitative support these findings, and to economically rationalise early intervention services in the UK.

title: Psychometric Validation of the Perceived Cognitive Assistance Scale: Exploratory and Confirmatory Evidence from LLM-Supported Decision-Making in Retail Trading
description: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as reasoning aids in complex decisions, but existing technology-adoption constructs do not directly capture decision-time cognitive support. This study validates Perceived Cognitive Assistance (PCA) - users' perceived expansion of cognitive capability during LLM-supported decision-making - in the demanding context of retail trading, because decisions are information-rich, uncertain, and consequential. Building on prior construct definition and content validation, we tested a nine-item PCA scale in two waves. Wave 1 used exploratory factor analysis in a pilot sample of LLM-using retail traders (N = 69). Wave 2 used preregistered confirmatory factor analysis and validity testing in an independent sample (N = 283). The one-factor model showed supportive fit on CFI (.966), TLI (.955), and SRMR (.049), with elevated RMSEA (.089). Reliability was strong (ω = .863–.883; α = .859–.881). PCA was adjacent to but empirically separable from perceived usefulness: HTMT was .845, the five-factor model outperformed a merged PCA-usefulness model, and ESEM reduced the inter-construct correlation to .775. PCA showed modest non-redundant association with complexity intentions, whereas the exploratory mediation pathway was not confirmed. Findings support PCA as a reliable measure of perceived cognitive scaffolding in LLM-supported decision-making.

title: Social disconnection, depression, and anxiety during parental leave among Slovak women
description:
Objectives
The study aimed to estimate the prevalence of social disconnection, depression, and anxiety among Slovak mothers on parental leave. The secondary objective was to assess the contribution of perceived loneliness and social isolation to depressive symptoms among mothers.
Methods
The final sample consisted of 410 mothers with a mean age of M = 33.4 and SD = 4.61. Data were collected online via battery of standardized self-report questionnaires, focused on depression, anxiety, perceived loneliness, and social isolation. 
Results
The prevalence rates of moderately severe to severe depression (PHQ-9 ≥ 10) and anxiety (GAD-7 ≥ 10) were 38.3% and 28.3%, respectively. The co-occurrence of both conditions was observed in 23.2% of mothers. Elevated levels of loneliness were reported by 78.3% of participants (UCLA-3 ≥ 6), while 26.8% were classified as socially isolated (LSNS-6 < 12), indicating a high prevalence of both loneliness and social isolation in this sample. Regression analysis showed that anxiety and loneliness were significant positive predictors of depressive symptoms, with the models explaining 61–62% of the variance.
Conclusion
The result of the study revealed elevated rates of depression and anxiety among mothers on parental leave. Psychological factors, particularly anxiety, loneliness, and social isolation, played a more prominent role in explaining depressive symptoms than demographic variables, including length of parental leave. The findings underscore the need for systematic screening and accessible perinatal mental health care among mothers on parental leave.

title: Learning while scaling: A dual-engine architecture for governing adaptation as evidence-based interventions scale
description:
Background: Social-impact organizations scaling evidence-based interventions face a tension: rigorous causal evidence accrues over years, yet organizations must adapt delivery in real time to fit new contexts, workforces, and budgets. Adapting without knowing which components are essential risks a "voltage drop"—the silent erosion of the mechanisms that made an intervention work. Implementation science offers many frameworks and methods, but they are largely applied to organizations by researchers rather than usable by organizations as ongoing practice.
Methods: We propose the dual-engine learning architecture, an operating framework that organizations can use to govern adaptation while scaling. We describe its three integrated elements and a five-step methodology for applying it, and illustrate each step with Shamiri, a tiered, lay-delivered mental-health model for adolescents in Kenya that has scaled to more than 250,000 youth.
Results: The framework has three elements. A dual-engine architecture runs two processes in parallel: an Evidence Engine that generates high-certainty causal knowledge through confirmatory designs, and an Optimization Engine that improves delivery through rapid embedded experiments. Certainty zones classify each component by the evidence a change requires—Zone A (load-bearing core), Zone B (directionally validated), Zone C (operational)—routing it to the appropriate engine, with escalation rules as evidence accrues. Prove–Improve–Move lifecycle phases coordinate the engines' shifting emphasis. Applied to Shamiri, the method decomposed the model into roughly fifty components (only three load-bearing); its Evidence Engine confirmed effects across a decade of randomized trials, and its Optimization Engine, over two implementation years, made delivery leaner and cheaper—each change non-inferior on adolescent depression and anxiety—while routine monitoring confirmed effects held at scale. 
Conclusions: The dual-engine framework gives organizatio

title: Autistic adults integrate the timing of beat gestures  and spoken prosody
description: In face-to-face communication, speakers provide several visual cues that facilitate speech comprehension for the listener. For example, they commonly make up-and-down flicks of the hand, called “beat gestures”, to emphasize a specific part of their speech (e.g., syllables, biasing the perceived lexical stress in words like CONtent vs. conTENT). These cues contribute to audiovisual speech integration, where listeners combine auditory speech with visual information from the speaker. Early research suggests that autistic children may show attenuated audiovisual speech integration effects (i.e., a reduced McGurk effect). However, more recent findings with adults show similar integration of speech and lip movements or meaningful gestures as non-autistic individuals. The present online large-scale study tested whether the timing of beat gestures biases audiovisual stress perception in autistic (N=101) and non-autistic (N=65) adults. Beat gestures affected speech perception to a similar extent in autistic and non-autistic adults. No relationship was observed between audiovisual integration and autistic traits (Autism Quotient-28 scores) or the use and understanding of visual communicative signals and gestures in daily life (Action and Feelings Questionnaire scores). Overall, these results converge with recent findings challenging the notion that audiovisual integration is altered in autism, at least in adulthood.

title: Continuous Traumatic Stress in Ukrainian Civilians: A Study of War-Affected Populations in Ukraine and Austria
description:
The Russo-Ukrainian War (RUW) led to encompassing and detrimental
consequences for millions of Ukrainian civilians abroad and in their war-torn homeland.
Recent representative studies found considerable psychological consequences, suggesting
high prevalences of mental disorders, and war-related distress alike. Continuous traumatic
stress (CTS) may constitute a valid construct describing psychopathological symptoms such
as depression, anxiety, PTSD, and DSO in war-affected Ukrainian civilians. This study
provides modest empirical evidence to the construct of CTS and the psychological distress
Ukrainian civilians face following the RUW. In the cross-sectional online study N = 113
datasets of Ukrainian civilians (M = 40 years; 87.6% female) in Austria (n = 67) and Ukraine
(n = 46) were collected in 2025. Associations of CTS with psychopathological symptoms, the
influence of emotion dysregulation (ED) on these associations, and differences in symptom
levels between two subsamples were examined. CTS predicted between 28% and 47% of
the variance in psychopathological symptoms. Preliminary findings showed small moderation
effects of ED on the association between CTS and psychopathological symptoms. Contrary
to hypotheses, participants in Austria reported higher symptom levels than participants in
Ukraine. Exploratory analyses revealed high prevalences of questionnaire-derived diagnoses
throughout the sample. Findings support the construct of CTS and its clinical value. Empirical
evidence indicates higher psychological distress in internationally displaced Ukrainian
civilians. High associations between CTS, ED, and psychopathological symptoms highlight
the need for qualitative and longitudinal data. Implications for trauma-focused research are
discussed.
Keywords: continuous traumatic stress, psychopathology, Ukrainian civilians, Russo-
Ukrainian War, cross-sectional study

title: Emergent categorical structure from sensorimotor grounding in a full-size human conceptual system
description: Categorical structure in semantic memory has long been assumed to emerge spontaneously from commonalities in the way we perceive and interact with the world around us. However, there are a number of outstanding questions about the scalability, generalisability, and grounding of the mechanisms and representations involved in current theories. In the present work, we examine whether the latent patterns in multidimensional sensorimotor experience produce an emergent categorical structure at the full scale of human semantic memory (40,000 concepts). We model the gradual organisation of conceptual knowledge in a novel, developmentally plausible, unsupervised hierarchical clustering framework, where earlier-acquired concepts provide the foundations of emerging structure that are further refined by later-acquired concepts. From sensorimotor experience alone, the cluster model produced a sophisticated emergent hierarchical structure. A four-way split of top-level domains (food/eating, using the body, rest of the world, abstract) suggests that the abstract/concrete distinction is not a simplistic binary division in semantic memory. Moreover, the cluster model’s emergent structure was able to distinguish between traditional semantic categories in both concrete (e.g., animals, tools, musical instruments) and abstract domains (e.g., emotions, professions, family relationships), but with some remaining difficulties (e.g., distinguishing fruit vs. vegetables, animals vs. birds). These findings demonstrate that a remarkable degree of complex semantic organisation emerges across the breadth of semantic memory solely from sensorimotor experience – without abstracted dimensions or features, or support from language, sociality, or emotional affect – thus providing an essential baseline against which the influence of these other sources of information can be evaluated.

title: Social Gaze Elicits Social Learning
description: Social gaze is a fundamental cue that regulates approach and avoidance in social interactions, yet it remains unclear how individuals learn from others’ eye gaze behavior. Using reinforcement learning modeling, we examined how micro-level gaze behaviors shape social preferences and closeness. Participants repeatedly chose between two interaction partners who predominantly (70%) responded with either direct or averted gaze and rated their closeness toward each partner. Results showed increased choice frequency and higher closeness ratings for the direct-gaze partner relative to the averted-gaze partner. Computational modeling revealed that others’ gaze direction generated a learning signal updating the latent value of interaction partners. This signal was calibrated by individual differences in how rewarding direct gaze was perceived. Initial attractiveness and valence of the interaction partner predicted this reward calibration, indicating that pre-existing impressions shaped how gaze feedback was valued. In turn, calibration was reflected in participants’ social evaluations of the partners, demonstrating how learning from gaze experiences contributed to impression formation. Together, these findings show that even minimal gaze interactions can shape social preference and closeness through reinforcement learning mechanisms, highlighting eye contact as a powerful but individually calibrated social signal with implications for social engagement in everyday and clinical contexts.

title: The Interplay Between Cognitive-Behavioural and -Interpersonal Factors in Disordered Eating: A Network Analysis
description:
Eating disorders (ED) involve distressing cognitions related to weight, shape and eating, as well as
disordered eating behaviours. The transdiagnostic cognitive-behavioral and cognitive-interpersonal
models are leading frameworks used to understand ED pathology. Although these two models highlight
complementary maintenance mechanisms, they have not yet been evaluated simultaneously. The current
study aims to integrate the core cognitive-behavioral and cognitive-interpersonal dimensions using
network analysis to examine their interrelationships across the spectrum of ED severity.
Method
A partial correlation network was estimated in a sample of Australian community/students
reporting ED symptoms (n = 493; n = 271 were above the Eating Disorder Examination Questionnaire Short
(EDE-QS) clinical cutoff; n = 108 reported a lifetime ED diagnosis). We evaluated topological overlap, node
predictability, and expected influence (EI) and bridge EI (BEI). Bootstrapped stability and centrality
difference tests were conducted.
Results
The network had excellent stability (all CS = 0.75). Central nodes were Concern Over Mistakes (EI
= 1.48), Impulse/NonGoals (EI = 1.41) and Overvaluation of Weight and Shape (EI = 1.35). Concern Over
Mistakes was the most significant bridge node (BEI = 0.86). One of the strongest edges in the network was
between Overvaluation of Weight and Shape and Weight and Shape Rigidity (edge = 0.45).
Conclusions
Our results support the robust and significant role of Concern Over Mistakes as a central ED
symptom. The high bridge centrality of Concern Over Mistakes indicates that it may serve as a pathway
connecting symptom clusters. Rigid beliefs about weight and shape may maintain disordered eating
symptoms.

title: Did you hear what you said? Inattentional deafness of pitch shifts in self- produced vocalization
description:
In everyday life, we normally do not pay attention to the sensory consequences of our own
actions such as the sound of our own voice. In some situations, however, such as when
required to speak a language we are not adept in, our attention is strongly drawn to our
own voice. Yet, even though attention is one of the most thoroughly studied phenomena in
cognitive psychology, and known to strongly modulate behavior, relatively little is known
about how attention influences the processing of sensory consequences of our own
actions. We show that when participants’ attention was sufficiently strongly directed to a
visual task, many individuals (50% out of 34) failed to notice that the pitch of their own
voice was unexpectedly and significantly pitch shifted (75 cents; the individuals had no
trouble detecting the pitch shift when they knew to expect it). The participants who
noticed the pitch shift made transient adjustments to the pitch of their voice, whereas the
“inattentionally deaf” participants produced longer lasting (up to 800 ms after the pitch
shift) adjustments to their voice. Our results demonstrate that our awareness of our own
voice can strongly depend on if we attend it or not, which can have significant
consequences on feedback motor control.

title: Flexible Multiple Imputation of Missing Data in Time-Structured Longitudinal Designs
description: Missing data are common in longitudinal designs and are often addressed with multiple imputation (MI), either as single-level MI, which treats repeated measures as separate variables, or multilevel MI, which treats repeated measures as nested within participants. Previous research has shown that both approaches can perform well in time-structured designs but has largely focused on growth modeling applications, where the assumptions underlying multilevel MI were met. In the present article, we argue that single-level MI is a more flexible method for handling missing data in time-structured designs that requires fewer assumptions and can accommodate a wider range of analyses than multilevel MI. In this context, we also consider applications of single-level MI to longitudinal multiple-indicator designs, in which single-level MI can be extended with composite scores or dimension reduction techniques such as partial least squares (PLS) to accommodate the potentially large number of variables in these types of designs. Our results from two simulation studies suggest that single-level MI provides a flexible treatment of missing data and that PLS in particular can facilitate single-level MI in applications with many variables. We conclude by discussing implications for applied research and by illustrating the application of single-level MI in an empirical example.

title: Psychosocial Wellbeing Assessment Tools for Children with Cerebral Palsy and Their Families:  A Rapid Review
description: Cerebral palsy is a lifelong motor diagnosis which is primarily associated with impaired movement, balance, and posture. Early intervention programs are widely encouraged to alleviate the physical, psychosocial, social and economic burden of these functional challenges. Despite the benefit of these early interventions, there are no standardised quantitative assessment measures or guidelines as to how to measure the benefits. We conducted and now report a rapid review of the applications of quantitative psychosocial wellbeing assessment tools for children with cerebral palsy under six and their families between 2017 and 2025. This rapid review was done as part of a charity, funded project to measure and assess their early interventions. After searching PsycINFO, Medline, CINAHL databases we identified and extracted data from 113 articles and assessed the utility of 34 free to access psychometric tests identified within them. While this review helped us to identify measures for our charity project, we also identify inconsistencies with reporting theoretical models and the factor validity of current measures, critique the lack of transparency in the data collection procedures reported and commiserate the number of inaccessible, paywalled measures that impair the measurement and assessment of wellbeing of children with cerebral palsy and their families.

title: Too anxious to learn:  Induced state anxiety prevents the formation of nocebo hyperalgesia
description: Nocebo hyperalgesia is characterized by a pain increase following an inactive intervention. Existing research on the mechanisms of nocebo effects has predominantly centered around the role of cognitive processes, leaving emotional processes relatively understudied. A recently proposed theoretical model on the role of negative emotions in the development of placebo effects in pain suggests that heightened anxiety may enhance nocebo hyperalgesia. The objective of the present study is to investigate the impact of pretreatment and posttreatment anxiety on the magnitude of nocebo hyperalgesia induced through classical conditioning. Healthy volunteers (N=120) were randomized to one of three groups: 1) classical conditioning of nocebo hyperalgesia preceded by anxiety induction (ANC); 2) classical conditioning of nocebo hyperalgesia (NC); and 3) no-manipulation control (CTRL). Anxiety was induced by the anticipation of an electric shock. During the conditioning, a placebo was paired with high-intensity heat pain stimuli, while moderate-intensity heat pain stimuli were provided without a placebo. Pretreatment anxiety disrupted the formation of nocebo hyperalgesia: although nocebo hyperalgesia was found in the NC group, no effect was observed in the ANC group. Nocebo conditioning changed the pain-related expectancies in the NC group: participants expected more pain than the CTRL group. Nocebo conditioning did not cause the development of posttreatment anxiety. Neither pain-related expectancies nor posttreatment anxiety mediated nocebo hyperalgesia. Because pretreatment anxiety influenced the formation of nocebo hyperalgesia, healthcare workers may consider patients’ anxiety level when treating pain as it may predict treatment responses.

title: Auditory Time-to-Contact in the Wild.
description: Understanding how the brain processes sensory information to guide goal-directed behavior has been predominantly investigated in controlled laboratory settings. However, ecological environments are characterized by noise, multi-causality, and probabilistic relationships between multisensory sources that may substantially modulate observed behavioral performance. In the present study, we implemented an auditory Time-to-Contact (TTC) task in a wild setting using an in-situ design aboard a French high-speed train (TGV), while participants were exposed to parametrically controlled ecological motion (Forward vs. Backward). Specifically, this setup was intended to manipulate the precision of available sensory information (Poor vs. Reliable) to investigate the flexible reweighting of sensory sources as a function of signal reliability. To understand what drives behavioral performance, we used Bayesian modeling to analyze the accuracy and precision of TTC estimates. This approach dissociates the respective contributions of internal references, sensory precision, and the ecological context. The results demonstrate that the reweighting of sensory sources is not simply driven by task-relevant signal precision but is differentially mediated by the specific conditions of the environment. Mainly, rather than a uniform environmental effect, we observed that ecological consistency situation (Forward motion) overrides task-goal requirements and suppresses the integration of incoming sensory evidence, leading to a systematic behavioral bias. Conversely, the unecological situation (Backward motion) results in behavioral decoupling from the environment, with performance returning to laboratory levels. Together, these findings suggest that internal model inference is highly context-dependent, where ecologically congruent environments can hierarchically dominate task accuracy.

title: Assessing chronic pain processes longitudinally: Psychometric evaluation of the German “Assessing Chronic Pain Processes” (ProCEss) item set
description:
Background. Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) is gaining relevance in contemporaneous pain research. However, EMA questionnaires are rarely psychometrically evaluated, potentially compromising data quality. This study aimed to develop and evaluate a German item set assessing psychological chronic pain processes for use in EMA, focusing on variability and reliability. 
Methods. First, the item set was piloted with three people suffering from chronic pain to gather feedback on participant burden and the usability of the data. Second, an evaluation was conducted with a sample of n = 68 individuals with lived experience of chronic pain who completed an EMA protocol with six assessments per day over twelve days (4,236 datapoints). Third, revised parts of the item set were preliminarily evaluated in n = 10 people suffering from chronic pain over a 21-day EMA period with six assessments per day (1,253 datapoints). Variability was examined primarily using within-person standard deviations and response distributions. Reliability was assessed according to current recommendations for single-item measures (test-retest reliability), two-item scales (Spearman-Brown coefficient), and multi-item scales (McDonald’s Omega).
Results. Most items and process scales met the predefined criteria for variability and reliability. However, variance was largely attributable to between-person differences. Four out of thirteen scales demonstrated insufficient reliability.
Conclusions. This study presents the first psychometrically evaluated German EMA item set capturing a broad range of clinically relevant pain processes. While the findings are promising, further refinement of several scales and additional psychometric evaluation are warranted.

title: Designing, Evaluating, and Using Synthetic Participants in Behavioural Public Policy: The PATH Checklist
description: Large language models are increasingly used to generate synthetic participants that simulate human responses to surveys, experiments, and policy proposals. This approach is attractive for behavioural public policy because it is fast, scalable, and inexpensive, and it can support early-stage exploration, pre-testing, and policy development. However, rapid adoption has outpaced the development of shared standards for evaluating when synthetic participant evidence is credible, useful, and appropriate for decision-making. We examine key limitations and unresolved questions in synthetic participant research and use them to develop PATH, a practical checklist for designing, evaluating, and using synthetic participant studies, organised around four principles: Purpose, Accuracy, Transparency, and Human grounding. The checklist is intended to help: (1) researchers design more transparent and robust studies, (2) reviewers and (3) academic readers evaluate the credibility and limits of the evidence, (4) policymakers judge when synthetic findings are sufficiently validated to inform decisions, (5) general readers interpret claims without overstatement, and (6) stakeholders ask the right methodological questions of commercial providers. We argue that synthetic participants can be useful in various contexts, but their value depends on validation quality, methodological transparency, and the purpose for which they are used.

title: Using features of dynamic networks to guide treatment selection and outcome prediction: The central role of uncertainty
description: Multivariate time series models are commonly used in psychology to investigate person-specific associations between multiple variables. They are often represented and interpreted as dynamic network models, where features such as the centrality of nodes can potentially guide treatment selection and outcome prediction. Researchers typically rely on point estimates of specific network features while ignoring estimation uncertainty, which can lead to wrong inferences and over-optimistic claims.  We introduce a one-step Bayesian approach for estimating multilevel vector autoregressive models (BmlVAR), which enables uncertainty quantification for person-specific network features and the regression of external outcomes on such features. In a preregistered simulation study, we compare the new model with several popular methods for network estimation. We also apply all methods to empirical data to highlight their differences. Our simulation results indicate that all methods perform mediocrely in estimating different centrality measures in practically relevant settings. BmlVAR still outperforms the other methods in many simulation conditions, especially regarding the statistical power to detect associations with an outcome. However, estimating the model properly can be challenging with limited data. Overall, all methods require a lot of data or very large effects to produce reasonably accurate results. Thus, although centrality measures based on dynamic networks have been widely used, our simulation study suggests they are unlikely to work well for achieving major goals of interest, such as guiding treatment selection and outcome prediction. We provide a new model that incorporates estimation uncertainty into the modelling process, thereby protecting against premature conclusions.

title: Sustained Attention in the Presence of a Smartphone:  Does Reward Expectation Matter?
description: Empirical evidence on whether the presence of a smartphone reduces attentional resources is mixed, suggesting additional factors are involved. The current study examines one such factor, namely the presence (or absence) of reward expectations. In a lab experiment, 111 participants were randomly assigned to one of three conditions (no smartphone present, smartphone present, or smartphone present with induced reward-message expectation). They completed a Sustained Attention to Response Task (SART) before and after the manipulation. Results indicate that the presence of a smartphone reduced reaction-time performance on the SART, but that reward expectations did not lead to an additional performance decrement. Exploratory analysis indicated that individuals higher in online vigilance showed greater attentional disruption in both the smartphone presence and reward expectation conditions. Together, these findings suggest that smartphone-related attentional costs may depend more on reward history and stable individual differences than on momentary reward anticipation.

title: “Already Outdated and Still Under Review”:  Mapping the Landscape of Research on Adolescent Development and AI Chatbot Use
description: Artificial intelligence (AI) chatbots are increasingly embedded in adolescents’ daily lives, yet developmental science research struggles to keep pace with adoption. This mixed-methods study gathered developmental expert perspectives via a survey (n = 141 researchers and adjacent professionals working on issues related to youth and technology) and semi-structured interviews with a purposive subsample (n = 15) to inform a coordinated research and policy agenda on adolescent AI use. The study aims were to (1) identify priority AI use cases for future study, (2) identify priority research questions and methods, as well as barriers to future research, and (3) identify societal guardrails that developmental professionals recommend enacting now, while research is underway. Experts cautioned against vague "screen time" measures and agreed that nearly all use cases merit study but prioritized those with severe or irreversible immediate-term harms, AI literacy, AI as a social/relational actor, AI-generated (mis)information, and overreliance. No single methodological consensus emerged; participants advocated broadly for methodological pluralism and triangulation. Key barriers included the rapid pace of change in AI technology and use relative to academic timelines, limited industry transparency, funding gaps, and misaligned academic incentives. Proposed solutions included shared infrastructure, interdisciplinary consortia, and community partnerships. Experts underscored that responsibility for safe AI should not rest primarily on adolescents and families and identified three immediate guardrails: enforceable youth-centered regulation, developmentally informed product design, and multidimensional AI literacy. Findings clarify a preliminary direction for future research and advocacy and emphasize that coordination among researchers and between sectors is needed.

title: Impact of a Pre-Test Measurement of Alcohol Craving: Lessons From a Self-Replication Study
description:
A prior study during COVID-19 lockdown (Heck et al., 2024) examined whether a pre-test alcohol craving measurement influenced posttest assessment and tested its relationship with social desirability (SD) and demand effects (the consequences of participants trying to uncover the study’s aim). Results suggested that the first craving measurement increased its post-experimental levels in heavy drinkers, with no associations found for SD or demand effects. Engagement with the alcohol-related video was linked to craving. This study aimed to replicate these findings three years later outside the lockdown context.
A hundred and forty-two psychology students were randomly allocated to either a “pre-post” or a “post-only” craving assessment group. They watched an alcohol-related video. SD, demand effects, engagement, and alcohol consumption levels were also assessed. 
Several findings supported the robustness of effects observed in the initial study, while others diverged, offering new hypotheses. Heavier drinkers reported significantly higher craving levels following video exposure and craving correlated moderately with video engagement. No significant associations were found between craving and SD or demand characteristics. However, unlike the initial study, repeated measurements showed no significant effects or interactions with alcohol consumption.
Reanalysis of the original data highlighted a gender-based interaction, where the effect of the interaction between repeated craving measurements and alcohol consumption was significant in men but not in women. These results suggest partial replication of prior findings and underscore the need to further explore contextual and demographic influences on alcohol craving. Moreover, this study confirmed a significant role of video engagement in craving.


Contact e-mail: equertemont@uliege.be or m.heck@uliege.be

title: A longitudinal qualitative study of facilitators and barriers to care-experienced children’s wellbeing during the transition to secondary school
description: While school transitions can be challenging for all children, those with experience of maltreatment, including care-experienced children, face additional complexities linked to poorer wellbeing outcomes. This study explored the barriers and facilitators influencing UK care-experienced children’s wellbeing during the transition from primary to secondary school. Twenty-six care-experienced children and 25 caregivers from England and Wales participated through the ReThink Programme. Semi-structured interviews were conducted with both groups before and after the transition, one year apart, and analysed using a framework-based thematic approach. Three overarching themes were identified: children’s sense of belonging, relationships and emotional support; the extent to which systemic support met individual needs; and children’s personal resources, including coping strategies and self-perceptions. Findings highlight dynamic interactions between relational, systemic and individual factors shaping wellbeing for maltreatment-exposed children across this period. While some experiences mirror those of the general population, distinct challenges were identified requiring targeted policy and practice responses.

title: Initial indications of generative AI influence in linguistics research abstracts: Evidence from a large-scale lexical frequency analysis between 2018 and 2025
description: Generative AI and large language models (LLMs), such as ChatGPT, have transformed many working practices, including scientific writing. Previous large-scale studies have identified shifts in the use of certain stylistic markers, such as the word delve, in disciplines like biomedicine, engineering, and computer science, yet comparable evidence from the humanities remains scarce. Using lexical frequency analysis, we examined the prevalence of 28 AI-associated stylistic markers across 129,338 published abstracts from 592 linguistics journals between 2018 and 2025. Counterfactual modelling suggested that approximately 16.28% of abstracts published in 2025 exhibited frequencies of AI-associated stylistic markers above levels expected from pre-LLM trends (95% CI = 14.80%–17.74%). Furthermore, higher-prestige journals exhibited slightly greater AI-associated marker word frequency. Country-level differences indicated particularly higher AI-word usage in abstracts from Asia and the Middle East. While lexical frequencies provide only indirect evidence of LLM use, the findings suggest that linguistic patterns previously associated with LLM-assisted writing have become increasingly prevalent in linguistics publications.

title: Off-days have real-world consequences: Using high-frequency cognitive assessment to predict naturalistic driving
description: It is becoming common to measure cognition repeatedly to capture dynamic fluctuations over time. It is currently unknown the extent to which daily deviations in cognitive performance have implications for real-world behaviors or merely reflect short-term change of limited scope, such as assessment artifacts. Daily driving behavior was captured in 160 older adults using GPS tracking across 19,628 individual “trips”, defined as ignition start to ignition off. Automated processing pipelines quantified various aspects of each trip including distance traveled and frequency of “adverse” events such as speeding or hard braking. Participants completed daily assessments of cognition using a remote testing platform. Multilevel negative binomial models were constructed to relate daily cognitive performance to adverse driving events and adaptive behavior such as avoidance of nighttime driving, controlling for global ability, participant age, and distance travelled. Rigorous multi-model inference using AIC-based model averaging indicated that daily cognitive deviations received substantial model support (79-98%) in predicting adverse events and nighttime driving, relative to models that did not include a daily parameter. In contrast, global cognitive ability did not substantially improve prediction of either outcome. These results show that deviations in cognitive performance are not merely “noise”, as they have measurable implications for real-world outcomes. Importantly, it was not global performance that was important but deviations from typical performance. Thus, single-shot assessments of cognition may not be sufficient to make determinations about driving safety. More generally, our findings highlight the utility of repeated cognitive assessments in understanding how cognitive fluctuations may impact real-world behaviors.

title: Controlled real-world object generation for visual memory experimentation and theory-building
description:
Real-world objects are visually rich and semantically structured, but due to this complexity, theories of visual memory are generally tested with more tractable stimulus spaces, such as color. To address the challenge of studying real-world objects with high experimental control,
we used a novel generative artificial intelligence (AI) Concept-based Controllable Generation (CoCoG) model, grounded in human similarity judgments, to create systematically structured object spaces. We then combined CoCoG-derived embeddings with a computational model of memory to predict memory error distributions. Across seven online visual-working-memory studies (N = 1,019), we found that these stimuli can be used to investigate established phenom￾ena, such object-state versus -exemplar memory, and new phenomena, specifically memory for novel object morphs. These results build on recent work that bridges generative AI with cogni￾tive modeling and demonstrate how architectures like CoCoG can be used as a methodological and theory-constraining tool for systematically studying real-world visual cognition.

title: L2 Input Variability Compensates for Individual Differences in Working Memory: Evidence from Learners Post-Concussion
description: Previous research on second language (L2) learning often assumes that optimized input benefits all learners equally. The Aptitude–Treatment Interaction (ATI) framework suggests, however, that optimal conditions may depend on how input matches learners’ cognitive resources, such as working memory (WM). We investigate how temporary deficits in WM, caused by a recent concussion, may be compensated through the engagement of alternative learning pathways. In a Spanish construction learning task involving verbs of “becoming,” we manipulated input variability (skewed vs. balanced), hypothesized to differ in WM demands. Results showed that skewed input enhanced learning for participants with lower WM and a recent concussion, but reduced learning for those with higher WM and longer recovery time. The findings support models of alternative learning systems adaptively recruited according to individual resources and input conditions. We discuss implications for tailoring input in language learning and for accommodations in applied contexts.

title: Sensitive periods for childhood adversity and their impact on later life outcomes: A narrative review
description:
Rationale. Childhood adversity can impair mental and physical health later in life, and these deleterious effects may depend on sensitive periods for child abuse during which children are particularly vulnerable. The purpose of this narrative review is to scope previous research on sensitive periods of child adversity that are linked with negative later life outcomes.
Methods. Only peer-reviewed original research articles were included in the review. Relevant key information was extracted from the articles and themes were identified as repeated patterns of meaning-making that evolve around a similar interpretation of the research findings.
Findings. From the scoped literature on sensitive periods for childhood adversity, three themes were identified: (1) The earlier the childhood adversity in life the worse, (2) There are two sensitive periods that correspond to periods of change in development: early childhood and early adolescence. (3) Sensitive periods for child adversity are domain-specific and depend on the aspect of impaired psychosocial functioning.
Conclusions. Despite evidence for sensitive periods for child adversity, it is too early to describe normative, sensitive periods because previous findings were largely study-specific. More research is needed to investigate the impact of child abuse on various domains of psychosocial functioning later in life. More prospective, and longitudinal research is also needed because current findings are subject to retrospective memory bias. Overall, when child abuse occurred during the lifespan may be important information in optimising and personalising treatment options.

title: The impact of cash out availability on online betting behavior
description: The rise of online sports betting, facilitated by technological innovations such as the cash-out feature, has transformed the gambling landscape. While cash-outs (a facility provided by betting operators which allows gamblers to close a bet early before the final outcome of a sports event is known) have been suggested to intensify problematic gambling behaviors, empirical studies to date have been correlational, leaving a gap in determining causal evidence. The present Registered Report investigated the direct impact of the perceived availability of the cash-out feature on various facets of gambling behavior in an online experiment. Hungarian residents with an online gambling history (N=595) were informed that they would have the chance to gamble with real money on sports events. Following random allocation, half of the participants were told that they would have the option to cash out their bets before the outcome of the sports event is known, while the other half did not have this option. Overall, the findings provided evidence that cash-out availability did not influence bet placement, wager size, or bet risk among our sample. However, the low variance in the outcomes may have limited the ability to detect subtle differences. Consequently, the null results should not be taken as conclusive evidence of no behavioral effect.

title: Sensorimotor Learning and Error Correction in a Double-Step Mirror-Reversal Reaching Task (FlowTask): Proof of Concept in Young and Older Adults
description: Adaptability and flexible error correction are essential to interact with dynamic environments. However, previously learned habitual motor patterns can hinder performance under altered task demands. Mirror-reversal tasks provide a model for studying de novo motor learning, requiring suppression of prior motor plans and the formation of new mappings. Additionally, target jumps require movement replanning during execution. To our knowledge, no study has investigated how replanning following error correction takes place when target jumps occur under error-generating mirror reversal mapping. Here, we introduce the FlowTask, a modified double-step mouse-based reaching paradigm with left-right mirror reversal, designed to probe error detection, correction, and the flexibility of ongoing motor control. For this preliminary study, fifty-eight participants performed reaching movements under normal and reversed mappings while mouse kinematics were recorded at 100 Hz. Primary outcomes included initiation time (IT), movement time (MT), trajectory types and normalized online correction time. Moreover, using the position (to categorize correct vs. error trajectories) and velocity (continuous vs. broken) at the point of maximum absolute deviation from the ideal path, we validated a trajectory type categorization (correct continuous, correct break, error continuous and error break). Particularly, error breaks trajectories were more frequent in mirror reversed blocks and decreased with practice. Additionally, beyond reproducing expected results of de novo motor learning (absence of after effect, increased IT), as well as on the effect of aging (poorer overall performance with slower movement execution and initiation), results showed that older adults were more impacted by the reversal, with large improvements over trials, although not reaching younger adults’ performance. Error breaks and Sample entropy were the most sensitive measures to mapping changes and aging, as they detected

title: The Morality of Partiality: How Relationship Expectations Shape Moral Evaluations
description: While many ethical traditions praise impartiality as a moral ideal, it remains unclear whether people view impartial actors as possessing an especially strong moral character. In the present research (N = 3,417), we examined how people evaluate partial and impartial actors who prioritized either a socially connected other (i.e., a family member, friend, or community member) or a stranger in need. Across classic sacrificial dilemmas (Experiment 1) and more ecologically valid helping scenarios (Experiments 2-3), partial actors were judged to be more moral and trustworthy than impartial actors. This preference was strongest when impartial actors failed to prioritize a close (e.g., family member or friend), rather than a more distant social tie (e.g., community member). Across experiments, impartial actors were also perceived as less predictable and as fulfilling less of their relational obligations, and these perceptions predicted judgments of morality and trustworthiness. In a fourth experiment, participants exhibited a preference for partiality in their own helping decisions, with a majority choosing to help a socially connected other over a stranger in greater need. This preference was stronger when decisions were described as publicly observable rather than anonymous, consistent with anticipated reputational consequences influencing helping decisions. Together, these findings suggest that everyday moral judgments serve a socio-relational function. Rather than evaluating moral character primarily in terms of adherence to abstract ethical principles, such evaluations appear to reflect assessments of individuals’ reliability as long-term social partners.

title: Voluntary Musical Imagery Use in Musicians’ Mental Practice: Functional Orientations and Pedagogical Implications
description: Mental practice is widely recognized as a useful strategy in musicians’ learning and performance preparation, with voluntary musical imagery (VMI) offering one means through which musicians may rehearse and organize music internally. Yet less is known about how musicians use VMI within routine mental practice. To investigate this, semi-structured interviews with 15 classically trained instrumentalists were analyzed using qualitative content analysis. Findings indicate that participants described VMI as an analytical, self-regulated practice strategy. This strategy was organized around two functional orientations: music-task-focused VMI and performance-situation-focused VMI. The former directed imagery toward musical material, while the latter directed imagery toward the broader conditions of performance. VMI use was reported as enhancing performance confidence, musical understanding, and memorization. However, participants also reported adverse experiences, including intrusive imagery or anxiety when it was difficult to verify imagined work physically, as well as constraints on VMI use related to cognitive overload, time pressure, emotional state, and limited pedagogical guidance. Taken together, the study offers a use-oriented understanding of VMI in naturalistic mental practice and suggests that music education may support VMI use through clearer task definition, strategy instruction, and attention to affective regulation.

title: EEG dynamics during prolonged Samatha meditation:  A case study across levels of meditation expertise
description: This case study examined differences in the temporal dynamics of EEG indices as a function of meditation expertise using a quantitative analytic framework. EEG was recorded and analyzed across multiple sessions over three days during prolonged Samatha meditation in two meditation novices and two expert meditators. Alpha-band power, beta-band power, and peak alpha frequency were extracted in 1-min bins and analyzed using linear mixed-effects models. These models revealed significant expertise × time interactions for beta-band power and peak alpha frequency. Within-group analyses showed that beta-band power decreased over time in the novice group, whereas beta-band power and peak alpha frequency increased over time in the expert group. These findings suggest that EEG indices during prolonged Samatha meditation may exhibit temporal dynamics that differ according to meditation expertise. The results also highlight the utility of analyzing EEG during meditation as a temporally unfolding process rather than only as session-level averages. In addition, a supplementary analysis using an external dataset was conducted to compare the present findings and to examine the applicability of the analytic approach. The supplementary analysis revealed patterns that differed from those observed in the primary dataset. This discrepancy underscores the need to consider factors such as meditation style, practice duration, participant characteristics, and measurement context when interpreting inconsistent findings in meditation EEG research.

title: Coherence Across Tasks In the Development of Counterfactual Reasoning
description: Counterfactual reasoning -- the ability to consider what could, would, or should have happened alongside what actually occurred -- is foundational to causal understanding, learning from experience, and making sense of others’ minds. While this skill emerges in early childhood, its developmental trajectory remains debated. How does this ability develop and what other constructs is it related to? To investigate, we explored the development of counterfactual reasoning through a 14-item unmoderated online battery measuring counterfactual reasoning across narrative, physical, and causal-machine domains in 320 children (ages 3-7). We found steady improvement on the tasks, with performance improving over early to middle childhood. Performance was also positively correlated within all three tasks, controlling for age and performance on non-counterfactual cognitive tasks, suggesting that all three domains were tapping into a similar underlying counterfactual ability. Additionally, children who responded correctly on narrative counterfactuals that could not be solved using heuristic shortcuts performed better across the other counterfactual tasks (Study 1) and Theory of Mind tasks (Study 2). These findings provide evidence for a latent construct underlying counterfactual reasoning and introduce a validated tool for future research.

title: Gender differences in Academic Stress and Academic Pressure among students aged 18-23 in Sri Lanka
description: Nearly 75% of students experience academic stress and pressure across the globe. There can be gender differences in perceiving and managing academic stress. The present study explored to identify the gender differences among the school and college students who experience academic stress and academic pressure. The sample consists of 58 students in total aged 18-23, 39 female students and 19 male students. They were chosen by purposive and snowball sampling method and the data was collected by an online based survey using the Google forms. Students were assessed with; Demographic questionnaires, Academic Stress Scale (ASS) and Academic Pressure Scale (APS). The collected data was analysed using IBM SPSS 25 version. To assess the hypotheses; Pearson correlation, Independent t-tests and ANOVA test were conducted. The findings of the study revealed that almost 45% of the participants in Sri Lanka experience academic stress and there is a strong positive correlation between academic stress and academic pressure. But, there were no gender differences among the sample in academic stress and academic pressure. Also, there were negative significant relationships between other demographic variables and academic stress. The students stated the major academic stressors were high workload, peer pressure and worrying about grades or results. Thus, every student who experience academic stress are prone to psychological disorders, there should be implications developed in order to maintain a stress-free, positive school and college environments.

title: The Nature of Mind-Wandering to Secrets.
description: Secrets are on people’s minds more than they are held back in conversation. Research suggests that such mind-wandering to secrets can be problematic for well-being, yet the mechanisms behind this link are still unclear. To better understand what may underlie the harm of mind-wandering to secrets, we conducted two studies. First, in a 14-day daily diary study (Study 1, N = 240) we investigated what people think about when mind-wandering to their secrets, and how these thoughts come to mind in daily life—spontaneously or deliberately. Worries and concerns about secrets intruded on the mind spontaneously, whereas when people intentionally mind-wandered to a secret, they fantasized and daydreamt about it (Study 1). Second, in a 7-day experience-sampling study (Study 2, N = 207) we probed the directionality of relationships between the two types of mind-wandering and affective experiences about secrets. Across studies, when secrets came to mind spontaneously, but not deliberately, people reported feeling greater negative emotion both in the moment (Study 1 and 2) and approximately 2 hours later (Study 2). This work offers the first investigation into spontaneous and deliberate mind-wandering to the same target content in daily life, capturing the temporal dynamics between mind-wandering and emotional well-being. Our findings point to spontaneous mind-wandering to secrets as a primary player in the well-being costs of secrecy. By yielding insights into the way secrets weigh on people’s minds, this research suggests possible ways to manage the well-being costs of secrets in everyday life.

title: Daily fluctuations in delay discounting and smartphone-based multimodal measures track nighttime drinking in alcohol use disorder: A 28-day monitoring study
description:
Background and Aims:
Alcohol use disorder (AUD) involves day-to-day fluctuations in cognitive, psychological, and contextual factors that are difficult to fully capture using retrospective or cross-sectional assessment alone. Computational markers, such as discounting rate, ambiguity tolerance, and risk preference, may provide useful indicators of day-level drinking risk, but have rarely been assessed repeatedly in real-world settings or examined together with daily app-based self-reports and passive smartphone-derived measures. This study examined whether these multimodal day-level measures were associated with same-day nighttime drinking, focusing on whether within-person changes in computational markers remained associated with drinking after accounting for concurrent self-reported and contextual measures.

Design:
A 28-day longitudinal observational study using repeated smartphone-based assessments and passive sensing.

Setting:
Daily-life smartphone-based monitoring of community-recruited participants in the Republic of Korea.

Participants:
The final analytic sample included 143 adults meeting DSM-5 criteria for AUD.

Measurements:
The primary outcome was same-day nighttime drinking, assessed using daily app-based self-reports. Daily predictors covered three domains. App-based self-reports included mood, alcohol craving, meal intake, and sleep-related variables. Computational predictors were derived from delay discounting and choice under risk and ambiguity tasks. Passive smartphone-derived predictors included time spent at home, calls, steps, and foreground app use. Generalized linear mixed-effects models examined associations between nighttime drinking and multimodal predictors decomposed into within-person and between-person components.

Findings:
A multimodal model integrating computational parameters, daily app-based self-reports, and passive smartphone-derived measures showed the highest explanatory power for same-day nighttime drinking (marginal R² = 0.

title: On Why Meta-d′ May Not Go the Distance: Confidence Distance as a Calibration-Based Measure of Metacognition
description: Meta-d' is widely used to quantify metacognitive sensitivity, and the meta-d'/d' ratio, commonly referred to as the M-ratio, is interpreted as metacognitive efficiency. However, both depend on assumptions inherited from signal detection theory, including Gaussian evidence distributions and a shared latent evidence axis for type-1 decisions and confidence judgments. These assumptions are not guaranteed in many behavioral paradigms. The present paper examines how violations of these assumptions affect meta-d' and the M-ratio, and introduces Confidence Distance as a calibration-based measure of metacognitive performance. Confidence Distance is defined as the mean absolute distance between trial-wise confidence and objective correctness after mapping confidence ratings onto the unit interval. Across simulation studies, non-Gaussian evidence distributions distorted the M-ratio even when metacognitive monitoring was ideal. These distortions were not limited to underestimation: heavy-tailed and light-tailed evidence inflated the ratio, whereas skewed evidence reduced it. In addition, when confidence was based on evidence not used for the type-1 response, meta-d' itself became inflated despite unchanged metacognitive ability. Confidence Distance, by contrast, remained comparatively stable across distributional forms, increased monotonically when metacognitive noise was added, and remained well-defined across multidimensional and multi-alternative task structures because it requires only trial-wise correctness and confidence. These findings suggest that meta-d' and the M-ratio conflate metacognitive performance with distributional misspecification or evidence-axis mismatch in applied research contexts. Confidence Distance therefore offers a transparent and assumption-light index of confidence–accuracy correspondence.

title: Syllables, not words, minutes, or utterances, to accurately characterize verbal input to children
description: The verbal input that children hear from caregivers around them can explain some of the well-known variability in trajectories of early child language development. Verbal input has traditionally been quantified by counting words, minutes, or utterances of child-directed speech from an adult interlocutor. This paper argues that exclusive reliance on these units can result in inaccurate estimation of verbal input, especially when comparisons are made between different languages, sociocultural settings, and speakers. Using spontaneous speech data from children and adults, we illustrate some confounds that these units create. Though illustrated with children’s verbal input, these confounds apply to any quantification of language structure across speakers, registers, or languages. We show that words, which vary in internal grammatical complexity between and even within speakers and individual languages, result in cross-linguistic and speaker age confounds. Utterances are likewise confounded with typological differences and speaker age. Minutes, which appear to be language-neutral, are in fact confounded with speaking rate which similarly varies within and between speakers, languages, and registers, especially since children speak slower than adults. We put forth measures based instead in information theory that employ the syllable as the base unit to resolve many of these issues: total information transmitted (syllable count x information density per syllable) and information rate (information density x syllable rate). These results demonstrate that accurate characterization of children’s verbal input requires paying close attention to linguistic structure and speaker attributes, and that information-theoretic measures can provide more reliable estimates across languages, speakers, and registers.

title: Trait Apathy and Fatigue are Associated with Diminished Academic Functioning Among University Students Aged 30 - 50
description: Motivation is critical to successful academic functioning. A growing body of evidence details how motivation evolves throughout the lifetime, yet middle adulthood is rarely examined. Here we examined how trait apathy and fatigue, both of which manifest in diminished motivation, relate to self-reported academic functioning among university students aged 30 – 50. 332 students completed trait apathy and fatigue scales, measures of academic functioning (autonomous motivation, controlled motivation, study engagement, study effort, academic stress, dropout intentions, academic performance, and study satisfaction), and measures of family, household, work-related factors. Apathy and fatigue were related to almost all academic measures, yet apathy was more closely linked to academic functioning; in particular autonomous motivation, study engagement, and study satisfaction. Our results suggest that relatively stable individual differences in motivation among healthy adults, as captured by the measures of trait-apathy and fatigue, are closely linked to functioning in a critical area – academic development.

title: Using Classification Trees to Identify the Best Method in Monte Carlo Simulations: From Population Parameters to Observed Features
description: Monte Carlo simulations are widely used to compare statistical methods, but their findings are often difficult to interpret and rarely translate into practical guidelines. This gap arises for two reasons. First, method performance often depends on complex interactions among simulation factors that conventional summaries fail to detect. Second, performance is typically evaluated against population values that are unknown in practice, whereas applied researchers see only features of their sample data. We build on the classification tree approach to address both problems, adding two pruning strategies for the simulation context: a combined procedure that accounts for equivalent representations of the same data, and effect-size-based pruning that prevents large numbers of replications from inflating tree complexity. We illustrate it with zero cell correction strategies for estimating tetrachoric correlations.
