In Example 1, we reanalyze results from Choi and Wu (2026) using the original simulation factors as predictors, yielding clear decision rules that still rely on population values. In Example 2, we generate data under continuous simulation factors and construct predictors directly from observed 2X2 contingency tables, so the rules can be applied to real data. More broadly, the framework provides an approach for translating Monte Carlo comparisons of competing methods into practical guidelines.

title: Is GenAI Helpful for Writing? A Re-Analysis Using Robust Meta-Analysis (RoBMA)
description: A recent meta-analysis showed that genAI has a strong impact on writing. The current analysis instead shows that the effects are negligible after correcting for heterogeneity and publication bias.

title: Unpacking the Dual-Path Trust Mechanism Driving Students’ Continuance Intention Toward Campus GenAI: A Hybrid SEM–Machine Learning Approach within the S-O-R Framework
description: As generative artificial intelligence agents become embedded in higher education, understanding students' continuance intention (CUI) is critical. To address the underexplored mechanisms regarding how campus AI environments are associated with user trust, this study adopts the Stimulus-Organism-Response (S-O-R) framework to investigate a dual-path trust mechanism: AI System-like Trust (AST) and AI Human-like Trust (AHT). Through a hybrid methodology combining Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM) and non-parametric Random Forest classification, this study analyzes university students' CUI toward campus GenAI. The findings demonstrate that Information Quality (IQ) and Service Quality (SQ) are positively associated with both AST and AHT pathways. Conversely, Facilitating Conditions (FC) and Performance Expectancy (PE) do not exhibit independent linear associations within the simultaneous structural estimation. Rather than indicating an empirical absence of functional value, this configuration uncovers a holistic evaluation mechanism among digital natives, where basic functional affordances operate as baseline hygiene constraints whose variance is subsumed by quality attributes. Notably, while both AST and AHT exert concurrently significant linear driving forces on CUI within the structural path framework, the exploratory machine learning analysis clarifies that under recursive partitioning, AHT exhibits a higher relative predictive weight. Furthermore, a localized asymmetry operates within the linear mediation channels, where SQ selectively mobilizes AHT over AST to drive continuous usage. Post-hoc inspections imply that PE operates through a non-linear threshold dynamic, transitioning into an active predictive catalyst primarily within the positive spectrum of user perception. Our study contributes to campus AI services evaluation by highlighting that within the contemporary GenAI ecosystem, students' evaluative focus prioritizes relational and

title: Let there be information: rethinking cultural evolution
description: Prevailing accounts of cognitive evolution and cumulative culture frequently rely on narrative reconstructions and analogical projections, extrapolating from contemporary human cognition to infer the mental capacities of extinct humans. While such approaches may illuminate recent cultural developments, their applicability to deep prehistory is limited. We propose an alternative framework grounded in the thermodynamic imperative shared by several past and present members of the genus Homo: the reduction of entropy in organism–environment interactions. Central to this framework is the concept of Zones of Bounded Surprisal (ZBS), which formalises adaptive cognition in terms of the capacity to register, tolerate, and integrate new (surprising) information. Rather than treating cultural accumulation as having been continuous, uninterrupted and progressive, ZBS foregrounds information as a driver of evolutionary dynamics relevant in natural selection. We argue that stone-age vagaries of cultural evolution in Homo should be considered from the standpoint of the transmissibility of information between preliterate human beings — with variably evolving ZBS — under different demographic, social, and material constraints. This account foregrounds the role of ZBS in adaptive engagement with novelty and epistemic affordances by extant and extinct Homo.

title: On cross-modal commutativity in magnitude production for loudness and brightness
description: Can the loudness of a tone be compared to the brightness of a light? The answer is affirmative. We appear to be intuitively capable of making such cross-modal comparisons. Psychophysical research has long assumed that these comparisons are mediated by a common scale of subjective intensity shared across modalities. R.D. Luce (2002, Psychological Review, 109, 520-523) developed a psychophysical theory of magnitude estimation and production based on this assumption and derived empirically testable conditions. In particular, he identified cross-modal commutativity as a key property for empirically evaluating the theory. In an extended, high-powered replication of an experiment reported by W. Ellermeier, F. Kattner, and A. Raum (2021, Attention, Perception, & Psychophysics, 83, 2955-2967), the present study examined this property using a cross-modal magnitude production task for loudness and brightness. Relative to the previous study, individual-level analyses yielded more substantial evidence both for and against crossmodal commutativity, while group-level results remained inconclusive. These findings are discussed in light of a generalized theory that predicts a near-miss to cross-modal commutativity while preserving the assumption of a shared representation of subjective intensity.

title: Development of the Japanese version of online computerized adaptive tests of young children’s vocabulary: Japanese CDI-CAT
description: Measuring children’s early vocabulary is crucial for developmental science and practical applications. The MacArthur–Bates Communicative Development Inventories (CDIs) are the most widely used caregiver-report checklist to assess children’s vocabulary across languages and dialects. Although full-length CDIs enable detailed analyses, completing checklists of several hundred words requires considerable time and effort from caregivers, often unnecessarily. Here, building on recent psychometric advances utilizing item response theory (IRT) in languages such as English and Spanish, we developed a computerized adaptive test of the Japanese CDI (Japanese CDI-CAT), which estimates total productive vocabulary size of the full-length CDIs from a reduced number of word items. Using a publicly available Japanese CDI dataset (N = 978) from children aged 12 to 38 months, we compared several IRT models and conducted simulations while varying the number of items administered (25–400). The results showed that the two-parameter logistic IRT model provided the best fit among the candidates. We also found that the CDI-CAT can successfully estimate children’s total vocabulary size while substantially reducing the number of items (e.g., r = .95 with the full-length CDI using only 25–50 items). By extending the CDI-CAT methodology to Japanese, we aim to improve the efficiency of vocabulary assessment and contribute to a more inclusive understanding of early language development across diverse linguistic and cultural contexts.

title: Successful coordination emerges from aligned self-predictions in a dynamic motor interaction
description: A central challenge in understanding joint action is explaining how individuals achieve successful coordination in dynamic, real-world settings. Although coordination is thought to depend on anticipating future events, including the actions of others, the precise contribution of such predictive processing remains unclear, since most existing evidence comes from simplified laboratory tasks that infer anticipation indirectly from reaction times. To address this gap, we studied 70 participants (forming 64 pairs) during solo and joint sessions of a turn-taking ball-hitting task while recording multimodal behavioral, physiological, demographic, and social measures. We found that successful coordination was most strongly associated with anticipation of one's own action outcomes, outperforming physiological synchrony, motor behavior, demographic characteristics, and social closeness. Partners whose predictions of their own action outcomes were more closely aligned coordinated better, likely because each behaved in ways the other implicitly expected. A Bayesian generative model further showed that well-coordinated pairs relied more heavily on prior expectations than on incoming sensory evidence when generating predictions during joint action. These results suggest that efficient coordination in dynamic real-world tasks emerges primarily when individuals' internal models for predicting their own actions are well aligned across partners. Our quantitative multimodal approach provides a framework for disentangling the contributions of predictive, physiological, motor, and social factors to coordination in naturalistic interactions.

title: From Design to Classroom: How VRT's EDUboxes Tackle Digital Media Literacy for Flemish Young People
description: The role of digital media in adolescents' lives is characterized by both opportunities and risks, raising the question of how young people can maximize benefits while minimizing harm. This challenge lies at the heart of digital media literacy (DML), commonly understood as comprising knowledge, attitudes, and skills related to the critical, responsible, and informed use of digital media. Despite assumptions that adolescents naturally acquire these competencies as “digital natives,” research shows substantial inequalities in DML, highlighting the need for structured support. Schools are widely recognized as key environments for fostering DML, yet they often struggle to implement it in a consistent and sustainable way. In Flanders (Belgium), DML is embedded across curricula rather than taught as a separate subject, resulting in fragmented responsibility and limited accountability. To support teachers, tools such as VRT’s EDUboxes have been developed. This chapter focuses on three EDUboxes addressing social media, artificial intelligence (AI), and sexting, each representing distinct but highly relevant domains of adolescents’ digital experiences. These topics illustrate how DML requires an understanding of platform logics, critical evaluation of emerging technologies such as generative AI, and the ability to navigate complex social and ethical situations online. This chapter examines how these EDUboxes are designed to foster DML by analyzing their underlying educational logic. Using a Theory of Change (ToC) approach combined with in-depth interviews with developers, we explore the assumptions, intended outcomes, and pedagogical strategies embedded in these tools, and assess how they align with insights from scientific and grey literature.

title: Lexize-SWE: A quick online test to assess print vocabulary knowledge in Swedish
description:
Vocabulary knowledge correlates strongly with general language skills, which is why vocabulary tests
have been used as a proxy for language proficiency. Recently, tests in several languages have been
developed to quickly and reliably assess vocabulary knowledge by means of the visual lexical decision
task. However, such a test does not currently exist for Swedish, a language that is not only spoken in
Sweden, but also by a large minority group in Finland. Lexize-SWE was designed to address this gap.
Lexize-SWE is a 10-minute online test that consists of 52 words and 26 pseudowords. Item performance
was tested with L1 and L2 speakers from both Sweden and Finland (N = 441), followed by a validation
with a new sample (N = 342). The results indicate that the test distinguishes between L1 and L2 speakers.
In L1 speakers, the scores were predicted by age and self-ratings in Swedish. L1 variety (Sweden
Swedish vs. Finland Swedish) was not associated with the test scores. In L2 speakers, the test scores
were predicted by self-ratings, exposure, daily usage of Swedish, language status (L2 speakers with and
without immigrant background), and educational level. In addition, Lexize-SWE scores showed a
moderate correlation with an external Swedish vocabulary test (SweLT). However, the background
factors predicted Lexize-SWE scores more strongly than SweLT scores. Overall, Lexize-SWE appears
suitable for assessing print vocabulary knowledge, particularly in L2 speakers, and is applicable across
both Sweden and Finland contexts. The test is available for free use at https://lexize.org/.

title: Visual Perception in Elite, Fast-Paced Ball-Sport Athletes: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
description:
Sensory perception – the process through which information from the environment is obtained through the senses and interpreted into meaningful representations – may play a key role in elite sports performance. However, the role of domain-general sensory-perceptual functions in elite sport remains unclear. This systematic review and meta-analysis aimed to examine the literature on the sensory perception of elite fast-paced ball sport athletes. Specifically, it aimed to determine whether these athletes possess enhanced sensory perception compared to both sub-elite athletes and non-athletes and whether sensory perception is associated with sport performance metrics.
A systematic literature search of Scopus, Web of Science and PubMed was conducted following PRISMA guidelines, including only studies that examined low-level sensory-perceptual functions (e.g.  visual acuity and depth perception) via domain-general tasks. The review focused on elite athletes, as defined by the Participant Classification Framework (McKay et al., 2022).  Three-level meta-analyses with robust variance estimation were conducted on studies comparing elite athletes to both sub-elite athletes and non-athletes. Studies investigating associations between sensory perception and sport performance were described via narrative synthesis.
Twenty-five studies met the inclusion criteria. Eighteen studies compared the visual perception of elite athletes with sub-elite and/or non-athletes, five studies examined the relationship between visual perception and sports performance, while two studies did both. Of the 18 comparative studies, seven studies with non-athlete control groups and six studies with sub-elite control groups met the criteria for meta-analysis; the remaining studies were analysed via narrative synthesis. A large enhancement in visual perception was observed in elite athletes relative to non-athletes (Hedges’ g =1.02, 95% CI: 0.61-1.43, p < 0.01), equating to a 76% probability that a random

title: Breaking the Cycle: Rewiring Negative Symptoms in Schizophrenia Through Physical Exercise
description: Individuals with schizophrenia commonly exhibit debilitating negative symptoms, including avolition and anhedonia, which represent a significant barrier to engagement in goal-directed behaviours, including physical exercise. They are less physically active than the general population, yet physical exercise is known to improve their symptoms. This narrative review investigates the bidirectional relationship between negative symptoms and physical activity. We propose that negative symptoms hinder engagement in physical activity by altering the cost-benefit analysis of exercise. This is exacerbated by deficits in anticipatory pleasure and reward learning, underpinned by dysfunctions within dopaminergic and glutamatergic circuitry which are critical for motivated behavior. We hypothesise that physical exercise can rewire these deficits through enhanced dopamine transmission, modulation of glutamatergic and GABAergic systems, and reduced inflammation. Furthermore, grounded in learning theories and adopting a cognitive-behavioural perspective, this review establishes a theoretical foundation for developing targeted interventions designed to enhance physical activity engagement in this population. We highlight that it is critical to ensure that physical activity is experienced as less effortful and more pleasurable than expected. By generating positive prediction errors (when the experience of exercise is less effortful or more pleasurable than anticipated) exercise helps individuals re-associate it with positive outcomes. Cognitive changes coupled with the biological effects of physical exercise on reward neural pathways are likely to have a synergistic effect, promoting a virtuous cycle of sustained engagement and symptom improvement.

title: Forensic Outpatient Cognitive Understanding Study (FOCUS): A study protocol
description:
Background: There is growing evidence that cognitive dysfunction plays a role in the emergence and persistence of antisocial behavior, and it is increasingly implicated as relevant for risk assessment and treatment within the Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) framework. However, this evidence comes predominantly from prison and inpatient populations. Whether similar patterns hold in forensic outpatient settings remains largely unknown. Moreover, existing studies often rely on normative groups that are not matched for age, sex, and education, and tend to focus on single cognitive domains rather than administering comprehensive test batteries.
Methods: This protocol paper describes the design and methods of the FOCUS study, which aims to map cognitive functioning in forensic outpatients and examine its associations with aggression and treatment outcomes. The study compares forensic outpatients (n = 100) to a normative group (n = 100, matched on sex, age, and educational level). All participants complete a comprehensive computerized neuropsychological test battery covering seven cognitive domains: intelligence, attention, language, executive functioning, social cognition, memory, and decision making. Aggression is assessed using both a self-report questionnaire (Reactive Proactive Questionnaire; RPQ) and a behavioral task (Competitive Reaction Time Task; CRTT). We have four research aims: (1) to compare the cognitive profile of forensic outpatients to a matched normative group; (2) to examine the correspondence between self-reported and task-based aggression; (3) to examine associations between neuropsychological test scores and aggression; and (4) to examine associations between neuropsychological test scores and treatment outcomes, including recidivism risk, treatment non-attendance, dropout, and actual recidivism. 
Discussion: The FOCUS study is the first to administer a comprehensive neuropsychological test battery in a forensic outpatient sample with a matched normativ

title: Ecological momentary intervention fidelity: Qualitative findings from a habit degradation trial
description: Objectives. Intervention fidelity is critical for interpreting trial outcomes, yet fidelity in digital behaviour change interventions (DBCI) such as ecological momentary interventions (EMIs) is poorly understood. This qualitative study explored participant experiences of intervention fidelity (delivery, receipt, enactment) of a randomised controlled trial (RCT) testing habit degradation strategies including implementation intentions, and the effect of reward on degrading unhealthy snacking habits in daily life. This study aimed to deepen the understanding of intervention fidelity from participants’ perspectives, while also generating generalisable lessons for DBCI. Design. Preregistered semi-structured follow-up interviews. Methods. Twenty-two former RCT participants were purposively sampled for heterogeneity across intervention groups. Interviews were conducted online using personalised graphical elicitation materials. Data were analysed using template analysis, with initial coding informed by three fidelity domains: delivery, receipt, and enactment. Results. Eight themes were identified across three interacting fidelity domains. Delivery concerns included technical disruptions and cue selection that shaped reward delivery. Receipt challenges included mismatches between if-then plans and assigned strategies. Enactment themes revealed that participants generalised behaviour change beyond their if-then plan, pragmatically employed multiple strategies, and were influenced by physical, social, and internal factors. Reactions to reward features were mixed, self-monitoring through the app facilitated behaviour change, and some participants experienced snacking to shift to other situations. Conclusions. This qualitative fidelity study revealed participant-experienced processes—including pragmatic adaptation and self-monitoring reactivity—that typical quantitative fidelity metrics do not capture. These processes should be anticipated in EMI study design, and qualitative fi

title: Assessing the added value of process measures for studying gamified learning
description:
Background: Research on gamified learning typically focuses on assessing learning outcomes. The cognitive-affective processes occurring during learning, and their contributions to learning outcomes, remain comparatively understudied. However, illuminating these processes is essential for optimising digital gamified learning interventions.
Objective: This experimental study evaluates the added value of incorporating process alongside conventional outcome measures, to better understand how gamification may affect learning.
Methods: A total of 121 participants were randomly assigned to a gamified or a non-gamified version of an otherwise equivalent associative learning task. Biometric process measures (heart rate, electrodermal activity, gaze behaviour) were recorded throughout the task. Situated interest and self-efficacy were assessed repeatedly via self-report, alongside post-task cognitive and motivational outcomes. Differences between conditions were quantitatively analysed for both process and outcome measures.
Results and Conclusions: In this laboratory setting, motivational processes and outcomes were surprisingly similar across conditions. In contrast, cognitive process measures revealed a slower learning progress with gamified than non-gamified learning. This was reflected also in biometric process features revealing the extraneous processing demands induced by game design elements. Most importantly, the informativeness of specific biometric features varied by task stage, indicating stage-dependent relevance of different cognitive demands (e.g., encoding, recalling, sustained attention). These findings suggest that gamification’s influence on learning processes is contingent on the task’s cognitive and perceptual affordances and their temporal distribution, a consideration central for the design of individualised, real-time adaptive digital learning systems.

title: The Body Problem: Cautionary Notes on the Embodied Self
description: Recent years have seen a decisive turn in neuroscience toward embodiment, shifting focus from a brain-bound view of the mind to a more holistic one where the body is treated as constitutive of self and consciousness. While this turn has yielded valuable insights through studies on interoception, multisensory integration and bodily ownership, it also carries significant theoretical and ethical risks. This essay identifies the body problem: the risk of collapsing selfhood into bodyhood, with troubling consequences when applied to bodies that are marginalized or that otherwise do not meet the status quo. Tracing historical debates on mind–body relations, clarifying contemporary notions of the self and examining empirical findings on embodiment, it is argued that strong formulations of the embodied self risk reviving old reductions whereby “less body” implies “less self.” Such reductions threaten to undermine the value of persons whose bodies diverge from normative assumptions. Rather than rejecting embodiment research, this essay calls for caution against its uncritical extension, underscoring the need to guard against theoretical slippage that may entail harmful social consequences.

title: Can a yoga psychology intervention change primal world beliefs?
description: Worldviews play a central role in psychological well-being, yet little research has examined whether fundamental world beliefs (“primals”) can shift following psychological intervention. The present exploratory study examined whether a yoga psychology based 12-session long intervention was associated with changes in primal world beliefs and well-being amongst adults diagnosed with mild-to-moderate depressive disorder (N = 31). Using a single-group pre–post design, participants completed measures of well-being, depressive symptoms, primal world beliefs and Triguna. Results indicated significant improvements in well-being across multiple measures (all p < .001), accompanied by increases in positive primal beliefs (Safe, Enticing, Alive, and Good) and changes in Triguna (increased sattva alongside reductions in rajas and tamas). Informant ratings of triguna traits paralleled self-reports, suggesting observable change. Depressive symptoms also decreased significantly. Findings provide preliminary evidence that shifts in fundamental beliefs toward the world may occur, along with an increase in well-being.

title: Context Matters, Doesn’t It? The Role of Context in Everyday Emotion Regulation Strategy Use
description: A common maxim holds that “context is everything”. This perspective transformed the field of emotion-regulation, which evolved from understanding which regulation strategies people use, to understanding how strategy use varies by context (i.e., strategy-situation fit). Theoretical accounts suggest contextual features are crucial in guiding people’s selection of emotion-regulation strategies. However, empirical evidence is isolated in piecemeal studies with inconclusive results. To comprehensively explore context-dependent emotion-regulation, we need data on emotion-regulation across many contexts, at scale. Combining 11 experience-sampling datasets in an integrative analysis (NID=1,720, Nobservations=134,470), we examined how regulation strategy use varies as a function of three theory-derived contextual features—intensity, controllability, and social features (i.e., the presence and closeness of others). We found limited support that context matters for daily emotion-regulation strategy use. Our study provides a well-powered foundational step to test a theory central to emotion-regulation research, and highlights the complexity of capturing strategy-situation fit in daily life.

title: Resilience across pandemic phases: distinguishing psychosocial correlates from stress-buffering effects in youth
description:
Adolescence and young adulthood are sensitive developmental periods in which stress exposure increases. Youth differ in their capacity to maintain or regain mental health after stressful experiences, reflecting individual differences in resilience. Resilience resources can support mental health by promoting resilient functioning and/or by buffering the association between stress exposure and mental health. The COVID-19 pandemic provides a unique context to investigate these processes across changing stress exposures.
	We examined youth resilience before, during, and after the COVID-19 pandemic in large Dutch population-based samples, including youth aged 12-25 years before (N = 12,937, 58% female), during (N = 8,934, 58% female), and after the pandemic (N = 37,797, 62% female). We investigated whether loneliness, optimism, and self-rated health were associated with resilient functioning and, to test stress-buffering effects, whether they moderated associations between stress exposure and mental health. Resilient functioning was operationalised as better-than-expected mental health given stress exposure, based on wellbeing, depressive symptoms, and a combination. 
	Higher optimism, lower loneliness, and better self-rated health were consistently associated with higher resilient functioning across pandemic phases and operationalisations (p<.001). Moderation analyses showed inconsistent evidence that these characteristics buffered associations between stress exposure and mental health. 
	Psychosocial characteristics may help explain which youth function better in the context of stress, but do not consistently indicate who is less affected by stress. The results support a view of resilience as a complex and context-dependent process, rather than the result of single protective factors with similar effects across time and contexts.

title: Appreciating Ambiguity in Art: The effects of semantic distance and insight confidence on aesthetic appreciation
description: Previous work suggests that people tend to more strongly appreciate artworks that they subjectively perceive as more ambiguous. Yet, ambiguity of an artwork has remained difficult to quantify in a standardised and objective way. We introduce a computational, description-based measure to quantify the ambiguity of an artwork, operationalised as the divergence between viewers’ descriptions (semantic distance), alongside a metacognitive measure of insight confidence capturing how confident viewers are in their interpretations. In an online study (N = 123), participants viewed artworks for 6 seconds and either described them and rated their insight confidence, or they provided their subjective aesthetic appreciation by reporting their interest, emotional intensity, and cognitive affect.  Across artworks, greater semantic distance, which indicated higher ambiguity was associated with lower insight confidence. Contrary to preregistered predictions, higher ambiguity was also associated with lower aesthetic appreciation. In contrast, insight confidence showed positive associations with all three appreciation measures. These results show that within our online study aesthetic appreciation is related to how certain a viewer is about what they are viewing rather than the degree of ambiguity of an artwork. Methodologically, this approach provides a generalisable framework for quantifying ambiguity in open-ended responses and dissociating stimulus-driven ambiguity from observers’ metacognitive certainty.

title: Intertwining experience and evidence: how can co-created causal diagrams guide meaningful research questions about psychosocial processes in pain? A participatory co-design study
description:
Objectives: To develop and implement a co-creation process for common language and multi-stakeholder engagement to produce a shared causal framework that helps identify meaningful research questions in the context of psychosocial processes in pain and guide data modelling decisions.
Design: Participatory co-design study using facilitated workshops and iterative synthesis to develop a ‘Dream’ Directed Acyclic Graph (Dream-DAG). The Dream-DAG represented an idealised visual causal map unconstrained by available study measures or specific dataset limitations. We used a storm metaphor where different components of the storm (i.e., thunder or lightning) mirror the temporal and causal inference features in DAGs (i.e., exposure or confounding).
Setting: A UK-based multidisciplinary pain research consortium involving researchers, clinicians and public contributors.
Participants: 12 participants took part, including 4 experts by experience living with chronic pain and 8 professionals including a wide range of researchers (from quantitative and qualitative fields), clinicians and data scientists. Participants were invited because of their lived, clinical, methodological or data-related expertise relevant to psychosocial processes in pain.
Results: Through the workshops we developed a shared language for causal thinking, a co-created Dream-DAG, and an operationalised set of hypothesised pathways to inform future data modelling within pain research. The variables and pathways of interest identified as part of this process cover a wide range of concepts considered relevant to the development and maintenance of pain. This included individual factors such as anxiety and loss of identity, interpersonal factors such as disruption to relationships, and the broader role of social worlds. Importantly, the DAG mapped the order of influences and highlighted disruption to relationships as a potential mediating indirect pathway between pain-related experiences and ongoing pain impact. The

title: Perceived error climate in primary school classrooms: A multidimensional perspective
description: The way teachers address errors in the classroom plays a crucial role in shaping how students experience their errors, regulate their learning processes, and ultimately learn from them. To capture these dynamics, the concept of the perceived error climate has been introduced, describing the extent to which errors are positively perceived and treated as constructive learning opportunities by teachers and students. Prior research with secondary school students conceptualized the error climate as comprising eight subdimensions organized under a single higher-order factor (Steuer et al., 2013). However, this structure has not yet been confirmed in younger students, and a single second-order factor may not provide a sufficiently differentiated representation of the construct. To address these deficits, we examined the structure of the perceived error climate in primary school classrooms and proposed a novel second-order structure distinguishing between emotional and instructional support following errors. The study drew on a sample of N = 2,628 third- and fourth-grade students from 187 classrooms across two domains (maths and German as a first language). Using multilevel multigroup confirmatory factor analyses, we examined the structure of the perceived error climate at both the individual and the classroom level. Results supported the proposed structure of eight subdimensions loading onto the two second-order factors of emotional and instructional support following errors. These second-order factors were positively related to students’ individual adaptive affective-motivational and adaptive action-related reactions to errors, respectively. Overall, the findings demonstrate that primary school students already perceive the error climate as a multifaceted classroom feature and that these distinctions are relevant for students’ engagement with their own errors.

title: Integrating gaze into models for multi-attribute pricing
description: To assess subjective value and form different types of judgments, people dynamically allocate attention to different attributes of their available options. Despite the development of models that characterize how people dynamically allocation attention during choice, the role of attention in producing other judgments, like pricing, is not well understood. Consequently, it is difficult to explain why they often result in differing apparent preferences. To address this, we measured attention during a multi-attribute pricing task where participants assigned willingness-to-pay prices to stimuli consisting of payoffs, delays, and probabilities, and during a multi-attribute choice task where participants chose between such options from the same stimulus set. Participants showed a strong preference for looking at the payoff attribute first in the pricing task, while the choice task resulted in attribute-wise comparisons that are not possible in pricing paradigms. To account for this behavior during multi-attribute pricing, we developed a model that first anchors on a payoff, and then adjusts to a final price based on information that is accumulated while attending to corresponding attributes. Empirical and modeling results taken together indicated that early anchoring (initial fixations on payoff) as well as architectural differences in information search (within-option vs between-option) produce the observed patterns of preference reversals, necessitating unique attention dynamics and models for choice and pricing tasks.

title: Traditional progressive tests with stopping rules from Computerized Adaptive Testing: Do potential advantages outweigh added complexity?
description: Progressive tests, where items are administered in order of increasing difficulty and test administration is stopped after the respondent has made a prespecified number of errors, are commonplace in psychological and educational practice. The Wechsler intelligence test batteries consist, for instance, of subtests that are based upon this classical adaptive testing format. Stopping a progressive test once frequent errors start to occur makes intuitive sense as the test would, by construction, only get more difficult. Yet, it is unclear how a stopping rule in terms of the number of errors translates into the desired measurement precision and accuracy, and it remains questionable whether stopping everyone after the same number of errors is warranted. In contrast, in modern computerized adaptive testing (CAT), tests are stopped on an individual basis as a function of measurement precision and/or accuracy. In this study, we assess the suitability of three popular CAT-based stopping rules (Change-in-Point-Estimate, Fixed-Precision, and Precision-Gain rules) for use in progressive tests and compare their performance to traditional consecutive-errors stopping rules. The extent to which CAT-based stopping rules can be translated into the simple but transparent count-of-errors metric of traditional stopping rules was explored. Results are discussed from the perspective of the differences in design and test adaptivity between progressive tests and CATs. We conclude with general considerations and implications for optimizing stopping strategies in progressive tests.

title: People have an accurate folk theory of conspiracy believers
description: Seeing someone endorse a conspiracy theory could be informative of which other conspiracy theories they might endorse and reveal broader personality traits. Across three pre-registered experiments (N = 775 US participants), we show that these inferences are supported by the nested structure of conspiracy beliefs: individuals who endorse less popular theories tend to endorse more popular ones, but not vice versa. This pattern holds for real-world conspiracy theories (false and true) as well as for invented ones. When presented with the information that someone endorses or rejects a conspiracy theory, participants could predict which other theories that individual would endorse or reject. Moreover, participants could infer the personality traits of conspiracy believers (e.g., narcissism, manichaeism) by appropriately weighing the rarity of the endorsed conspiracy theory. These findings suggest that people use a folk theory of latent conspiracism to draw broadly accurate inferences about conspiracy believers (or non-believers) from limited information.

title: When Felt States Become Social Evidence: An Identifiability Framework for Hot-State Social Inference
description: People often treat their feelings as evidence about what another person means or intends, but the same judgment shift can reflect valid cue use, response threshold, projection, attentional amplification, or self-state use. This article develops hot-state evidentiary admission theory (HEAT), a middle-range framework for ambiguous, action-relevant social-state inference. HEAT specifies when a self-state becomes target evidence: when it is bodily salient, encounter-plausible, and poorly differentiated from target and context. Its key prediction is source-sensitive cue reweighting. When a self-state is admitted, the self-state and state-consistent global cues should gain weight, ambiguous diagnostic cues should lose weight, and clear diagnostic cues should retain or regain influence under correction. HEAT separates mean shifts from cue utilization and reframes debates in sexual misperception by specifying when feelings function as useful evidence, misleading evidence, or neither.

title: Frequency and perceived plasticity of climate-relevant consumer behaviors as a function of physical and social factors
description: Large-scale changes in consumption are essential for mitigating climate change. Yet, people’s behaviors and their perceptions of what is feasible to change are deeply influenced by their social and physical environments. Despite growing recognition of these influences, empirical evidence integrating both current behavior and perceived feasibility of change remains limited. In this study, we examine the relationship between perceptions of physical and social environments and both the frequency of high-impact transport and food behaviors, as well as their perceived behavioral plasticity—the perceived likelihood of changing such behaviors while considering associated costs and barriers. In exploratory analyses, we also examine how perceived behavioral plasticity relates to perceived wellbeing impacts of behavior change. Using survey data from 1,202 UK adults, we analyzed current driving, air travel, public transport use, and red and white meat consumption, together with perceived plasticity and wellbeing impact for each behavior. Social environmental factors, particularly descriptive norms and family goal support, predicted most current behaviors and perceived plasticity across domains. By contrast, perceptions of physical environmental factors, such as public transport access or food appeal, were associated with behavior and plasticity in more domain-specific ways. Perceived behavioral plasticity was positively correlated with perceived wellbeing impacts, suggesting that perceptions of feasibility and desirability are intertwined. These findings together demonstrate the importance of targeting both physical opportunity structures and social contexts when promoting climate-relevant behavior change.

title: The social ecology of development: The changing structure of preschoolers’ everyday experiences
description: Everyday social interactions shape human psychological development. Research, however, has primarily relied on controlled settings or brief observations; consequently, the nature of naturalistic everyday experiences remains largely unstudied. To address this gap, we developed an automated multimodal processing pipeline to analyze a large egocentric corpus of naturalistic everyday experiences from 107 children aged three to five years in Germany (267.52 hours). The pipeline detects faces, persons, proximity, and speech types to classify time into interacting, available, or alone states. Results indicate a significant ecological reorganization: social interaction dominates at age three and declines towards a more even distribution between states at age six. Conversational agency remains stable throughout preschool, with children sharing it nearly equally with conversational partners. Crucially, social interactions change in their structure in that isolated social cues become significantly less predictive of interactions with age. Thus, preschool social interactions become less frequent but more diverse with development.

title: Intercorrelations of Paraphilic Interests in a General Population and a Pedophilic Subsample
description: Previous studies have found positive associations between a wide range of paraphilic interests, but recent research on a sample of users of child sexual abuse material suggested that pedophilic interest may be unrelated to other paraphilic interests. In the present study, we investigate the intercorrelation of paraphilic interests in a representative sample of the general population of the United States. Network analysis indicated that paraphilic interests, including pedophilic interests, were positively associated with each other and could be meaningfully grouped based on consent.  However, when examining a subsample of people exhibiting at least some pedophilic interest, the association between pedophilic interest and other paraphilias was substantially weaker. Further research with specially recruited populations will be necessary to explain this phenomenon, but we speculate that this observed difference between the full sample and pedophilic subsample may be explained by a related third variable (e.g., hypersexuality).

title: Perspectives et connaissances des mondes professionnels : un cours de carrière développant les compétences de hasard planifié
description:
#### FRENCH 
Selon la Théorie du Hasard Planifié, les événements imprévus dans les trajectoires professionnelles sont autant d’opportunités de carrières. Les individus gagneraient donc à être réceptifs aux événements imprévus, sinon même à les générer. Dans cette continuité, les conseillers d’insertion et d’orientation pourraient accompagner les individus à développer des compétences de hasard planifié (curiosité, persévérance, flexibilité, optimisme et prise de risque) leur permettant de reconnaître les possibilités offertes par le hasard, d’adopter des comportements propices à en engendrer davantage, et de transformer ces événements imprévus en opportunités de carrières. Nous créons, déployons et évaluons les effets d’un cours de carrière favorisant le développement de ces compétences de hasard planifié auprès de 214 étudiant·es en Licence 2 de psychologie de l’Université de Rouen Normandie. L’évaluation pré-post (janvier à avril-mai) montre une amélioration des cinq compétences de hasard planifié, et suggère un rapport plus clair et optimiste quant à leurs futures opportunités académiques et professionnelles. Une analyse en cluster montre que les effets de l’intervention sont d’autant plus positifs que le niveau de compétences de hasard planifié pré-intervention est faible. 

---

#### ENGLISH
According to the Planned Happenstance Theory, unexpected events in career paths are opportunities for career development. Individuals would therefore benefit from being open to unforeseen events, or even actively creating them. Building on this, career and guidance counselors could support individuals in developing planned happenstance skills (curiosity, perseverance, flexibility, optimism, and risk-taking). These skills enable them to recognize opportunities presented by chance, adopt behaviors that may generate such opportunities, and transform unexpected events into career prospects.
